docs.title: ⚡ Real-Time Bidding & Fraud Detection Platform
docs.text: This is a live auction platform that uses AI to catch fraudulent bids in real time. It is built with Spring Boot and Redis to handle fast, continuous data streams, instantly blocking bots without slowing down the user experience.

docs.title: 🏗️ How It Works
docs.text:
Here is a high-level look at how data moves through the platform when a bid is placed:

graph LR
    %% Define styles
    classDef user fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef core fill:#10b981,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef db fill:#ef4444,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef ai fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;
    classDef external fill:#374151,stroke:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff,font-weight:bold;

    %% Nodes
    User((User)):::user
    BE[Bidding Engine]:::core
    Redis[(Redis Pub/Sub)]:::db
    Sentinel[Sentinel Service]:::ai
    LLM[OpenAI]:::external

    %% Flow
    User -->|1. Places Bid| BE
    BE -->|2. Saves & Broadcasts| Redis
    Redis -->|3. Triggers Alert| Sentinel
    Sentinel <-->|4. Checks Context| LLM
    Sentinel -.->|5. Rejects Fraud| Redis
    Redis -.->|6. Live UI Update| BE
    BE -.->|7. Shows Rejection| User

docs.title: The Core Pieces
docs.text:
• The Storefront (Bidding Engine): This is the user interface. It takes incoming bids and uses active streams to update the screen instantly for everyone watching, without them needing to refresh the page.
  • The Broker (Redis): This acts as the central nervous system. Instead of the storefront talking directly to the security service, they both just talk to Redis. This keeps the storefront moving fast.
  • The Security Guard (Sentinel): This service silently watches every bid that drops into Redis. It packages the bid's context and asks the AI if the behavior looks like a bot. If it catches fraud, it sends a kill signal back through Redis to reverse the bid on everyone's screen.

docs.title: 🚀 Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Follow these steps to run the platform locally on your machine.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Docker (must be running in the background)
  • Java 21+
  • OpenAI API Key (needed for the Sentinel to analyze bids)

docs.title: 1. Get the Code
docs.text:
git clone [https://github.com/walker-systems/auction-system.git](https://github.com/walker-systems/auction-system.git)
cd auction-system

docs.title: 2. Start the Database
docs.text: docker compose up -d

docs.title: 3. Start the Bidding Engine
docs.text:
Open a terminal in the root folder and run: 

cd bidding-engine
./mvnw spring-boot:run

docs.title: 4. Start the AI Sentinel
docs.text:
Open a new terminal window, set your API key, and run the service: 

cd auction-system/sentinel-service
export SPRING_AI_OPENAI_API_KEY='sk-your-actual-key-here'
./mvnw spring-boot:run

docs.title: 5. Open the Storefront
docs.text: Go to http://localhost:8080 in your browser. * Click "Start Chaos" to unleash the demo bots. * Watch the AI intercept and reverse fake bids in real time.

docs.title: 🛑 How to Stop It
docs.text:
When you are done testing, you can easily clean up your environment:

  1. Stop the Apps: Press Ctrl + C in both terminal windows (or simply close the tabs).
  2. Stop the Database: Run this command in the root folder to shut down Redis safely: 
    docker compose down

docs.title: 🛠️ Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Port 8080 or 8081 is in use: If the apps fail to start, another background process might be using their ports. Find and kill the process: 
    lsof -i :8080
kill -9 <PID>
  • Maven Wrapper (./mvnw) fails: If hidden .mvn folders were lost during the download, regenerate the wrapper using your system's global Maven installation: 
    mvn wrapper:wrapper
  • App crashes immediately: Make sure you exported the SPRING_AI_OPENAI_API_KEY in the exact same terminal window where you are running the Sentinel service.

docs.title: 📬 Let's Connect
docs.text:
Justin Walker

  • 🌐 Portfolio: justin-castillo.github.io
  • 💼 LinkedIn: Justin Walker
  • 📧 Email: justinwalker.contact@gmail.com

docs.title: ADR-001: Use of Reactive Redis over RDBMS
Context
docs.text: Auctions experience sudden traffic spikes (10k+ TPS) in the final seconds. Standard relational databases (like Postgres) struggle to handle this concurrency without heavy scaling.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Spring Data Redis Reactive will be used as the primary database for active auctions.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Very fast read/writes (<5ms) and built-in support for atomic operations.
  • Cons: All active auction data must fit in server RAM.
  • Mitigation: Redis AOF (Append Only File) is enabled to back up data to the disk.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Domain objects in this bidding system mostly hold data, rather than complex business logic.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Java 25 Records will be used for all domain models.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Immutable and thread-safe by default.
  • Cons: Cannot use standard JPA (Hibernate) lazy loading. This is acceptable since Redis is used instead of JPA.

docs.title: ADR-003: Adoption of ReactiveRedisTemplate & JSON Serialization
Context
docs.text: Standard Spring Data reactive repositories do not support Redis. Additionally, the default JSON serializer is deprecated.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
1. Database access methods (Repository pattern) are manually written using ReactiveRedisTemplate.
  2. The newer RedisSerializer.json() is used for serialization.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Fully non-blocking I/O.
  • Cons: Requires writing custom code for standard database actions (save, find, delete).

docs.title: ADR-004: Using Mono.defer() for Reactive Database Mocking
Context
docs.text: When unit testing code that retries a failed database update, standard mock objects return the exact same data on every retry. This fails to simulate a real database where the data might change between retries.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Mono.defer() is utilized when mocking reactive streams that need to simulate changing states.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Tests correctly simulate the database changing during a retry loop.
  • Cons: N/A

docs.title: ADR-005: Decoupling Sentinel from Bidding Engine's Redis
Context
docs.text: Currently, the AI (Sentinel) and the main bidding system share one Redis database. Heavy AI data processing could slow down the core bidding system.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: (Planned) The Sentinel's state management will be moved to its own separate database. Redis will only be shared for real-time messaging (Pub/Sub).

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Prevents the AI from slowing down user bids.
  • Cons: Requires setting up and managing more database infrastructure.

docs.title: ADR-006: Providing the Sentinel a Feedback Loop
Context
docs.text: The AI currently evaluates bids without any way to learn from its mistakes (e.g., flagging a real user as a bot).

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: (Planned) AI decisions will be saved to an audit log. Admins will be allowed to correct mistakes, and this corrected data will be used to retrain the model.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: The AI gets more accurate over time.
  • Cons: Requires building an admin interface and a data feedback pipeline.

docs.title: ADR-007: Upgrading to Event Sourcing for Rollbacks
Context
docs.text: When a fraudulent bid is canceled, the database is currently overwritten. This deletes the record of who the previous valid bidder was.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: (Planned) The architecture will transition to Event Sourcing. Instead of just saving the "current bid," every action (bids, rejections) will be saved as an append-only log of events.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Makes it easy and accurate to undo fraudulent bids. Provides excellent historical data for training the AI.
  • Cons: Requires a major architectural rewrite.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Using a standard LLM via Spring AI is too slow and expensive for evaluating high-speed numerical data (like reaction times and bid amounts).

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: (Planned) The general LLM will be replaced with a specialized Machine Learning model (CatBoost) running on a Python FastAPI server.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Much faster processing and lower costs.
  • Cons: Adds Python to the tech stack, necessitating the management of a multi-language (polyglot) architecture.

docs.title: ADR-009: Decoupled ML Pipeline via Synthetic Data
Context
docs.text: Building a complex ML model and connecting a new Python server to the existing Java backend at the same time is risky. It can cause massive integration bugs at the end of the sprint.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: The network connection will be built and tested first using temporary, fake data and a simple AI model. Once the Java and Python servers communicate perfectly, the real, complex ML model will be swapped in.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Proves the network plumbing works early. Keeps the Java and Python code strictly decoupled.
  • Cons: Requires writing throwaway scripts to generate the fake data.

docs.title: ADR-010: Containerization Strategy for Python ML Microservice
Context
docs.text: Java applications often use automated Buildpacks (like mvn spring-boot:build-image) to create containers without manual setup. However, Python Machine Learning libraries (like CatBoost) require specific operating system dependencies. Automated tools often fail to guess and install these low-level requirements correctly.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: A standard Dockerfile will be used to containerize the Python ML microservice instead of automated Buildpacks.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Guarantees the production container exactly matches the local training environment.
  • Cons: Requires manual file maintenance and differs from the Java project's container strategy.

docs.title: ADR-011: Atomic Proxy Bidding via Redis Lua Scripts
Context
docs.text: High-concurrency proxy bidding (multiple users setting maximum bids simultaneously) causes race conditions if handled via standard Java read-modify-write operations across the network.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Core bidding logic, including tiered increment calculations and proxy bid resolution, is implemented as a custom Lua script executed directly inside Redis.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Guarantees atomic execution and prevents race conditions without the overhead of distributed locks.
  • Cons: Business logic is split between Java and Lua, making debugging and maintenance more complex.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Core database operations rely heavily on custom Redis Lua scripts. These scripts cannot be accurately simulated using standard Java mock objects (like Mockito) during testing.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Testcontainers is adopted for integration tests, spinning up ephemeral Redis Stack Docker containers during the test lifecycle.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Verifies actual database and script behavior against a real, isolated Redis engine.
  • Cons: Increases test suite execution time and requires Docker to be running on the host machine.

docs.title: ADR-013: Epoch Timestamps for Cross-Boundary Time Math
Context
docs.text: The anti-sniping "soft close" feature requires comparing and extending auction end times inside a Redis Lua script. Passing Java Instant objects natively caused serialization and comparison mismatches between JSON strings and numeric database formats.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Time thresholds are calculated as Epoch seconds in Java and passed to Lua scripts as numeric values for direct mathematical comparison.

docs.title: Consequences
docs.text:
• Pros: Eliminates date parsing errors and guarantees precise, cross-boundary time comparisons.
  • Cons: Requires manual conversion to and from epoch seconds when invoking database scripts.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Ship a production-minded proxy bidding engine with deterministic rollback, real-time SSE updates, and evidence (tests + metrics + docs).

docs.title: Working rules
docs.text:
• One ticket per branch: sprint3/-
  • Squash merge each ticket into main (via PR)
  • Keep diffs minimal; avoid refactors

docs.title: Execution order (recommended)
docs.text: 1) DATA-101 Active Auction Index (Replace KEYS scan) 2) RT-201 Per-Auction Pub/Sub Channels 3) RT-202 SSE Stream Endpoint 4) RT-203 SSE Coalescing (~100ms) 5) PB-302 Bid Increment Table 6) PB-301 Max Bid Endpoint 7) PB-303 Lua Proxy Bid Engine (HASH + ZSET, atomic) 8) PB-304 Fraud Rollback via ZSET recompute 9) OBS-401 Actuator + Micrometer 10) OBS-402 Custom Counters 11) OBS-403 Live Stats (logs or /stats) 12) QA-501 TESTING.md + docs/evidence/ 13) QA-502 Integration Tests (proxy bidding + rollback + SSE smoke) 14) DOC-602 ARCHITECTURE + DESIGN_DECISIONS refresh 15) DOC-601 README refresh

docs.title: DATA-101 — Active Auction Index (P0) — Est 1.0h
docs.text:
• Remove Redis KEYS usage for listing auctions
  • Maintain SET active_auctions
  • findAll uses SMEMBERS + fetch per id

docs.title: RT-201 — Per-auction Pub/Sub (P0) — Est 0.5h
docs.text:
• Replace global channel with auction:updates:
  • Ensure publish + subscribe both updated

docs.title: RT-202 — SSE Stream Endpoint (P0) — Est 1.0h
docs.text:
• Add endpoint /api/auctions/{id}/stream producing SSE
  • Streams updates for that auction id

docs.title: RT-203 — SSE Coalescing (P1) — Est 0.5h
docs.text: • Coalesce stream (e.g., sample latest every ~100ms)

docs.title: PB-302 — Increment Table (P1) — Est 0.5h
docs.text: • Implement tiered increment function

docs.title: PB-301 — Max Bid Endpoint (P1) — Est 1.0h
docs.text:
• POST /api/auctions/{id}/max-bids
  • Validates request and calls Lua engine

docs.title: PB-303 — Atomic Proxy Bidding Lua Engine (P1) — Est 4.0h
docs.text:
• Uses HASH state + ZSET max bids
  • Enforces increase-only replaceable max bids
  • Computes top2, visible price, updates state, increments version
  • Returns updated state for response + publish update

docs.title: PB-304 — Deterministic Rollback via ZSET recompute (P1) — Est 1.0h
docs.text:
• ZREM fraudulent user from max_bids
  • Recompute leader + visible price deterministically
  • Update state + publish update

docs.title: OBS-401/402/403 — Observability (P1) — Est 2.5h total
docs.text:
• Add actuator endpoints
  • Add counters: bids total, collisions, rollbacks
  • Provide live stats via periodic logs or /stats endpoint

docs.title: QA-501/502 — Testing & Evidence (P0/P1) — Est 3.5h total
docs.text:
• docs/TESTING.md + docs/evidence folder
  • Integration tests for Lua proxy bidding + rollback
  • SSE smoke test

docs.title: DOC-601/602 — Docs refresh (P0) — Est 2.5h total
docs.text:
• Update architecture + decisions docs to match implementation
  • README includes demo steps and evidence links

docs.title: Evidence locations
docs.text:
• docs/TESTING.md
  • docs/evidence/
    • tests-pass.png
    • sse-sample.txt
    • metrics-sample.txt or png

docs.title: ⚡ Bidding Engine
docs.text:
A High-Frequency, Event-Driven Auction System.

The Bidding Engine is a reactive microservice designed to handle concurrent bids with sub-millisecond latency. It leverages Redis Streams for event propagation and Optimistic Locking (CAS) to ensure data integrity during high-traffic auction closings.

docs.title: 🚀 Key Features
docs.text:
• Reactive Core: Built on Spring WebFlux (Netty) for non-blocking I/O.
  • Race Condition Handling: Uses Redis WATCH/MULTI/EXEC patterns to prevent double-spending.
  • AI Integration: Emits real-time events for Price Prediction models (via MCP).
  • Vector Search: Uses Redis Search to find similar auctions by semantic embedding.

docs.title: 🛠️ Tech Stack
docs.text: Component Technology Language Java 25 (Record Patterns, Reactive Streams) Framework Spring Boot 4.0 (WebFlux) Database Redis Stack (JSON + Search + Streams) Testing Testcontainers + JUnit 5

docs.title: 1. Start Infrastructure (Redis Stack)
docs.text: docker compose up -d

docs.title: 2. Run Application
docs.text: ./mvnw spring-boot:run

docs.title: Sentinel Service ⏲
docs.text: A high-performance, distributed rate limiting microservice built with Spring Boot WebFlux and Redis.

docs.title: ☰ Overview
docs.text:
Sentinel is designed to protect downstream services from being overwhelmed with traffic. Originally designed to accompany the "Bidding Engine".

Key Features * Distributed State: Limits/usage are shared across multiple instances via Redis. * Atomic Operations: Lua scripts prevent race conditions. * Reactive Stack: Built with WebFlux to leverage non-blocking performance. * Ready to Run: Fully dockerized.

docs.title: 🐳 Quick Start
docs.text:
1. Create compose.yaml:
services:
  sentinel:
    image: justinwalkerhub/sentinel-service:latest
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    environment:
      - SPRING_DATA_REDIS_HOST=redis
    depends_on:
      - redis

  redis:
    image: redis:alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
  1. Run it:

docker compose up

docs.title: 👩🏻‍💻 Development Setup
docs.text:
Prerequisites * JDK 25+ * Docker (for Redis)

  1. Start Redis

docker run -d -p 6379:6379 --name sentinel-redis redis:alpine

  1. Run the App

./mvnw spring-boot:run

docs.title: 🔌 API Usage
docs.text:
Check Rate Limit

GET /check

Returns 200 OK if allowed, or 429 Too Many Requests if blocked.

Parameters * capacity (int): Max Tokens in the bucket. * rate (int): Refill rate (tokens per second). * cost (int): Cost of this request. * Header X-User-ID: Unique identifier for the user.

Example Request: 

curl -v "http://localhost:8080/check?capacity=10&rate=1" \ 
-H "X-User-ID: test_user"

Response (JSON): 

{
    "allowed": true
}

docs.title: 📘 Documentation
docs.text:
Interactive API documentation (Swagger UI) is available when the app is running:

http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html

docs.title: 📐 Architecture
docs.text:
The Token Bucket Algorithm

  1. State: Each user (X-User-ID) has a "Bucket" stored on Redis.
  2. Refill: Tokens are added based on time elapsed since last request.
  3. Consume: If the bucket has enough tokens, the request is allowed (tokens decrement).
  4. Reject: If user doesn't have enough tokens, request is denied.

All of this happens inside a single Lua script to ensure atomicity. 

-- Simplified Lua Logic

local time_delta_seconds = math.max(0, time_now - time_of_last_refill)
local tokens_owed = time_delta_seconds * token_refill_rate
current_tokens = math.min(token_capacity, current_tokens + tokens_owed)

-- Code omitted

if current_tokens >= tokens_requested then
can_afford_request = true
current_tokens = current_tokens - tokens_requested
redis.call("HMSET", key, "current_tokens", current_tokens, "time_of_last_refill", time_now)
redis.call("EXPIRE", key, 3600)
else
can_afford_request = false
end

return { can_afford_request and 1 or 0, current_tokens }
